Section 23.15
DISACCHARIDES
Copyright © 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Disaccharides
Disaccharides give two monosaccharides upon acidic hydrolysis
Often C-1 (anomeric) in one sugar is linked via oxygen to a different carbon in the other sugar
Configuration at the free anomeric carbon can vary

Lactose
Lactose is similar to cellobiose, but differs in its configuration at C-4 in one of the sugar units

Sucrose
Sucrose does not undergo mutarotation (why?)
The enzyme invertase cleaves sucrose to glucose and fructose, generating invert sugar

Section 23.16
POLYSACCHARIDES
Polysaccharides

Polysaccharides-1
The amylose chain exhibits an interesting helical twist.

Polysaccharides-2
Amylopectin has a brush-like structure.

Section 23.17
APPLICATION OF FAMILIAR REACTIONS TO MONOSACCHARIDES

Monosaccharides Are Carbonyls
Aren’t these sugars mostly cyclized? Why do these reactions work?

Monosaccharides Are Alcohols
Monosaccharides are ultimately alcohols, too!
Previously encountered reactions of alcohols will mostly work on monosaccharides
For example, alkylation and acylation

Monosaccharides Are Alcohols-1
Monosaccharides are ultimately alcohols, too!
Previously encountered reactions of alcohols will mostly work on monosaccharides
For example, intramolecular acetal formation

Monosaccharides Are Carbonyls-1
Monosaccharides are ultimately aldehydes or ketones!
Previously encountered reactions of carbonyls will mostly work on monosaccharides
For example, enolization
Aren’t these sugars mostly cyclized? Why do these reactions work?

Monosaccharides Are Carbonyls-2
Monosaccharides are ultimately aldehydes or ketones!
Previously encountered reactions of carbonyls will mostly work on monosaccharides
For example, aldol addition and retro-aldol
Aren’t these sugars mostly cyclized? Why do these reactions work?

Aldose-Ketose Isomerization
The enol intermediate after enolization could be the enol of a ketone or aldehyde (it’s really an enediol )
Conversion back to the other possible keto form results in isomerization

Epimerization
Re-protonation of the enediol can happen from either face, leading to scrambling of configuration at C–OH

Section 23.18
OXIDATION OF MONOSACCHARIDES

Oxidation of Terminal Groups
The aldehyde and primary alcohol in open-chain sugars are easily oxidized
The resulting diacid is called an aldaric acid ; intermediate oxidation products are also accessible
Aldonic Acids: Synthesis & Lactonization
Uronic & Aldaric Acids

Periodate Oxidation
Treatment with sodium periodate gives two aldehydes via oxidative cleavage
Extensive protection of other –OH groups is essential

Section 23.19
GLYCOSIDES: SYNTHESIS OF OLIGOSACCHARIDES

Controlled Synthesis of Disaccharides
Fischer glycosidation seems applicable to 1 → n disaccharide synthesis, but what’s wrong with this idea?

Controlled Synthesis of Disaccharides-1
Fischer glycosidation seems applicable to 1 → n disaccharide synthesis, but what’s wrong with this idea?
All but one of the anomeric carbons and –OH groups must be protected

Steps for Controlled Glycosidation
Protect all hydroxyls on glycosyl donor (electrophile); replace anomeric –OH with leaving group
Protect all but one hydroxyl on glycosyl acceptor (nucleophile)
Perform nucleophilic addition of acceptor to donor
Remove all protecting groups
The Nucleophilic Addition Step

Section 23.20
GLYCOBIOLOGY

Glycoproteins
Carbohydrates are often linked to other groups in the cell
On the cell surface, they are used for recognition, signaling, and other “informational” applications
Different blood types are associated with different groups on the surfaces of blood cells.

Pathogenic Carbohydrates
Pathogens (viruses and bacteria) use unique carbohydrates in a similar capacity on their surfaces
Influencing these unique structures allows us to selectively undermine these pathogens!
For example, inhibition of a carbohydrate-cleaving enzyme in influenza undermines the virus
